name: Iran / Op. Epic Fury
parties: U.S. + Israel vs. Iran
sources: CSIS (Cancian & Park, Mar 2026) — $3.7B first 100 hrs, $16.5B by Day 12; Pentagon Congressional briefing (Military Times, Mar 2026) — $11.3B at Day 6; Penn Wharton Budget Model (Apr 2026) — total costs up to $47B if through end of April; Middle East Monitor (Mar 2026) — ~$27.5B through Day 32; CNN Politics (Mar 6, 2026) — daily cost breakdown; Pentagon supplemental budget request (Mar 19, 2026) — $200B for full campaign costs; White House (Apr 8, 2026) — ceasefire announcement; Iran International (Mar 31, 2026) — 4,700 IRGC/security forces KIA; Hengaw Organization for Human Rights (Apr 8, 2026) — 6,620 Iranian security forces KIA; NPR (Apr 8, 2026) — fragile ceasefire; CFR (Apr 2026) — Will It Last?; NaturalNews citing Pentagon (Apr 8, 2026) — ~$500M/day confirmed
summary: Operation Epic Fury launched Feb 28, 2026. Pentagon confirmed $11.3B in first 6 days (primarily munitions replacement — $3.1B in first 100 hours alone per CSIS). CSIS estimated $16.5B by Day 12. Sustained rate settled at ~$500M/day by Days 25–32. Total through Day 32 (Apr 1): ~$27.5B. On March 19, the Pentagon submitted a $200B supplemental budget request covering projected full-campaign costs. Penn Wharton Budget Model projected costs could reach $47B if operations continued through end of April. CEASEFIRE: The U.S. and Iran agreed to a two-week ceasefire on April 8, 2026 (Day 40), mediated by Pakistan and brokered via the Islamabad Accords. The White House declared core military objectives achieved in 38 days — more than 85% of Iran's defense industrial base, including the majority of its ballistic missiles and long-range drones, destroyed. Iran claimed victory, asserting it forced the U.S. to accept its 10-point plan (including lifting sanctions and U.S. withdrawal from regional bases); Trump called the 10-point plan a 'workable basis on which to negotiate.' The Strait of Hormuz was reopened under the ceasefire. Israel did NOT join the ceasefire — Netanyahu declared Lebanon operations continue separately. The cost counter is frozen at the ceasefire date (Day 40, ~$31B total). Note: concurrent 2026 Israel–Hezbollah/Lebanon operations tracked separately under 'lebanon2026'.
comparables: The total has already exceeded the Manhattan Project in today's dollars — reached in under 5 weeks of fighting
At $500M/day, this operation spends the WHO's entire annual global health budget ($6.8B) every 14 days
Each month of operations ($15B) could fully fund the World Food Programme for nearly 2 years

casualties.source: CENTCOM statements; Military Times; DefenseScoop; Al Jazeera death toll tracker (Day 34, Apr 2, 2026 — 2,076 killed, 26,500+ wounded); Iranian Red Crescent; The Intercept (Apr 1, 2026 — 'Casualty Cover-Up' allegation); Military.com (Apr 4, 2026 — DCAS: 365 WIA, 13 KIA); Iran International (Mar 31, 2026 — 4,700 IRGC/security forces KIA); Hengaw Organization for Human Rights (Apr 8, 2026 — 6,620 Iranian military/security forces KIA, 20,880+ injured); White House (Apr 8, 2026 — ceasefire); Al Jazeera live tracker Day 40 (Apr 8, 2026)
casualties.note: Figures remain contested at ceasefire. Iran has not published a verified military vs. civilian breakdown. Al Jazeera's live tracker on Day 34 recorded 2,076 total killed and 26,500+ wounded — this reflects all causes and is likely an undercount. Iran International (Mar 31) cited 4,700 IRGC/security forces KIA separately; Hengaw Organization put Iranian military/security killed at 6,620 by April 8 — far higher than official/media totals, suggesting significant military casualties withheld. The Intercept (Apr 1) alleged CENTCOM systematically undercounted U.S. casualties. DCAS shows 365 confirmed U.S. troops wounded, 13 KIA. The ceasefire took effect April 8 (Day 40) but was immediately complicated by continued Israeli strikes on Lebanon.

entries.label: Total killed in Iran (all causes, Al Jazeera Day 34, Apr 2)
Total wounded in Iran (Al Jazeera Day 34)
IRGC / Iranian security forces (KIA, Iran International Mar 31)
Iranian military / security forces (KIA, Hengaw Apr 8)
Senior Iranian officials killed
entries.count: Defence Minister, IRGC C-in-C, Supreme Leader Khamenei

entries.label: U.S. troops killed (CENTCOM confirmed through Apr 1)
U.S. troops wounded (DCAS/Pentagon, Apr 4)
entries.count: 365 confirmed (Army 247, Navy 63, Air Force 36, USMC 19); 520+ per independent reporting (The Intercept)

spendChart.note: Iran expenditure undisclosed — see cards below

parties.name: United States
parties.explainer: $3.7B in first 100 hours; $16.5B by Day 12 (CSIS/Pentagon). Rate settled at ~$310M/day as operations shifted from initial B-2 and Tomahawk strikes to sustained lower-cost munitions.

parties.name: Israel
parties.explainer: Estimated from IDF operational tempo and Bank of Israel emergency budget supplement. Figures are partially entangled with the concurrent 2026 Lebanon War and cannot be cleanly isolated — range reflects this uncertainty.

parties.name: Iran
parties.explainer: IRGC and military budgets are classified and not publicly disclosed. Known activity: 500+ missiles launched, nationwide air defence activated. No reliable cumulative figure can be modelled from open sources.

name: 2026 Lebanon War
parties: Israel vs. Hezbollah
sources: Times of Israel (Mar 2026) — NIS 143B defense budget + NIS 39B supplemental; Bloomberg Mar 2026 — war costs $1.6B/week for all Israeli ops; CFR (Mar 2026) — Israel plan to occupy southern Lebanon; ACLED Middle East Special Issue Mar 2026; Al Jazeera (Apr 8, 2026) — 254 killed in Operation Eternal Darkness; Washington Post (Apr 8, 2026) — Israel strikes central Beirut after Iran ceasefire; Times of Israel (Apr 8, 2026) — IDF launches largest airstrikes yet on Hezbollah
summary: Hezbollah resumed rocket fire against northern Israel on March 2, 2026 — breaking the Nov 2024 ceasefire — in response to Op. Epic Fury against Iran. Israel launched an expanded air campaign over Lebanon, followed by ground operations in southern Lebanon beginning March 16. By late March 2026 Israel's combined war costs (Iran + Lebanon) were running at ~$1.6B/week. $150M/day reflects Israel-specific Lebanon operational costs; anchor of $2B captures the initial bombardment phase. CRITICAL ESCALATION: On April 8, 2026 — the same day the U.S.-Iran ceasefire was announced — Israel launched 'Operation Eternal Darkness,' its largest single-day strikes of the entire 2026 Lebanon War: 50+ fighter jets, 160 munitions, 100+ air strikes across Lebanon including central Beirut without warning. Lebanon's Civil Defence reported 254 killed and 1,165 wounded in that single day. Netanyahu explicitly stated the Iran ceasefire 'does not include Lebanon.' Hezbollah halted attacks on Israel temporarily after the Iran ceasefire, but Israel continued operations. Total killed in Lebanon since March 2, 2026: 1,784+; wounded: 5,977+; displaced: 1.2M+. Concurrent U.S. and Israeli costs for the Iran campaign are tracked under Op. Epic Fury.
comparables: The $150M/day rate exceeds the UN peacekeeping force in Lebanon's (UNIFIL) entire annual budget ($490M) — every 3 days
One month of this conflict ($4.5B) surpasses all international reconstruction aid delivered to Lebanon since the 2020 Beirut port explosion
At this rate, a year of fighting would consume Lebanon's entire pre-war GDP ($18B) nearly three times over

casualties.source: Lebanese Ministry of Health / Civil Defence; IDF spokesperson (Times of Israel, Apr 2026); Al Jazeera (Apr 2, 2026 — 1,318 killed, 3,935 injured; Apr 8, 2026 — 254 killed in Operation Eternal Darkness, 1,165 wounded); Washington Post (Apr 8, 2026); Reuters (Apr 2026 — Hezbollah sources say ~400 fighters killed); Long War Journal (Apr 4, 2026 — IDF shift to South Lebanon Security Zone); Wikipedia 2026 Lebanon war; ACLED Middle East Special Issue Mar 2026; Al Jazeera humanitarian crisis report (Apr 7, 2026)
casualties.note: Active conflict — figures rising rapidly. Lebanese Civil Defence total on April 8 includes the deadliest single-day attack of the war: Operation Eternal Darkness killed 254 and wounded 1,165 across central Beirut and other areas — 50+ jets, 160 munitions, with no prior warning. Total war dead through April 8: 1,784+; wounded: 5,977+. Approximately 1.2 million people (~20% of Lebanon's population) have been displaced. IDF claims 700+ Hezbollah operatives killed; Hezbollah sources put losses at ~400 fighters. IDF soldier figures are confirmed only. Hezbollah halted attacks on Israel after the Iran ceasefire was announced, but Israel explicitly excluded Lebanon from the ceasefire and launched its largest strikes of the war the same day. Iran had demanded any ceasefire cover Lebanon — that condition was not met. The conflict remains ACTIVE with no ceasefire in sight for the Lebanon front.

entries.label: Total killed in Lebanon (Civil Defence, Apr 8 2026)
Killed on Apr 8 alone (Operation Eternal Darkness)

spendChart.note: Hezbollah expenditure undisclosed

parties.name: Israel
parties.explainer: Estimated from IDF operational tempo and Bloomberg's $1.6B/week figure for all Israeli ops. Lebanon-specific costs isolated at ~$150M/day, excluding concurrent Iran/Epic Fury expenditure.

parties.name: Hezbollah
parties.explainer: Hezbollah's operational budget is not publicly disclosed. Iran provides an estimated $700M–$1B/yr in total support (CSIS), a portion of which funds Lebanese operations — no reliable daily figure can be modelled.

name: Russia-Ukraine War
parties: Russia vs. Ukraine + Western support
sources: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database 2024–2026; SIPRI Insights 2026 — Budget for a Fifth Year of War; Al Jazeera Feb 2026 four-year review; CFR Ukraine Aid Tracker (Dec 2025); Kiel Institute; UkraineWorld (Russia 2026 budget analysis); Russia Matters War Report Card (Apr 8, 2026)
summary: Combined: Russia ~$165B/yr (SIPRI/UkraineWorld 2026 budget: 14.9T roubles, 6.3% of GDP) + Ukraine ~$71B/yr (2025 defense budget) + European aid ~$65B/yr = ~$301B/yr or ~$825M/day. Total Western aid committed: $188B+ (CFR, Dec 2025). US withdrew 99% of direct support in 2025; Europe increased contribution but has not fully compensated. Russia's military spending as % of GDP peaked at 7.5% in 2025, declining slightly to 6.3% in 2026 (14.9T roubles planned) — though budget amendments expected as higher oil prices from the Iran war ease fiscal pressure (SIPRI, 2026). As of April 2026, Russian forces occupy roughly 20% of Ukraine. During March 10–April 7, Russian forces gained 17 sq miles — slow incremental gains continuing.
comparables: The total cost is now the most expensive conventional war since WWII — the combined spending has already surpassed the Marshall Plan (in today's dollars, $160B) four times over
At $825M/day, this conflict burns through an Apollo Moon Program's worth of spending (in today's dollars, $280B) every 10 months
Russia's military spending alone ($165B/yr) now exceeds its entire federal healthcare budget for 145 million citizens — by more than 2×

casualties.source: BBC / Mediazona open-source investigation (Apr 3, 2026 — 207,552+ identified KIA); BBC modelled range (Apr 3, 2026 — 319,300–461,200 KIA); The Economist (Feb 2026 model); CSIS (Dec 2025 — 1.2M total casualties, 325,000+ deaths); UK MoD (Jun 2025 — 250,000 killed); OHCHR (Jan 31, 2026 — 15,172 civilians killed, 41,378 injured); Al Jazeera 4-year review (Feb 2026); Zelenskyy public statement (Feb 2026); Ukraine General Staff (Apr 1, 2026 — 1,298,730 total Russian casualties); SIPRI Insights (2026 — Russian 2026 budget analysis); Russia Matters War Report Card (Apr 8, 2026)
casualties.note: Russia does not publish military casualties. BBC/Mediazona open-source investigation identified 207,552+ Russian KIA by name as of April 3, 2026 — a confirmed floor. BBC modelled total Russian KIA at 319,300–461,200 (April 3, 2026). CSIS estimated 1.2M total Russian casualties (killed and wounded) through December 2025; Ukraine's General Staff tallied 1,298,730 by April 1, 2026. UK MoD (June 2025) estimated 250,000 killed. A late February 2026 Western estimate put Russian total casualties (killed + wounded) at ~1,000,000, lower than CSIS's figure — divergence likely reflects methodological differences. Ukrainian figures are state-suppressed; Zelenskyy cited 55,000 KIA in Feb 2026, widely considered an undercount. OHCHR civilian deaths: 15,172 confirmed killed, 41,378 injured by January 31, 2026. Russia is cutting planned defense spending by ~7% in 2026 (14.9T vs 16T rubles in 2025), but mid-year budget amendments are likely given higher oil prices from the Iran conflict.

entries.label: Russia (total casualties, Ukraine General Staff, Apr 1 2026)
Ukraine (total casualties killed + wounded, CSIS est.)

spendChart.note: rates estimated from SIPRI, Kiel Institute, IMF anchors

parties.name: Russia
parties.explainer: Federal defence budget grew from $65B (2021) to $165B+ annually, financed via oil revenues and wartime bonds. Spending as % of GDP reached 7.5% in 2025 — highest since the Cold War.

parties.name: Allied Military Aid
parties.explainer: Peaked 2023–24 via US USAI packages, EU European Peace Facility, and UK commitments. US withdrew 99% of direct support in 2025; European contributions increased but did not fully compensate. Kiel Institute tracked $188B+ committed through Dec 2025.

parties.name: Ukraine
parties.explainer: Domestic defence outlays rose from $6B (2021) to $71B (2025), financed by wartime taxation, IMF/World Bank budget support, and war bonds. Figures per Ukrainian Ministry of Finance and IMF Article IV consultations.

name: Gaza / Middle East Wars
parties: Israel + U.S. vs. Hamas / Hezbollah / Houthis
sources: Bank of Israel (direct defense costs ~$77B through 2025); AA.com — war expenses $76.3B in 2 years (Oct 2025); Brown University Costs of War 2025 — U.S. military aid $21.7B; Breaking Defense Mar 2026 — Israel $45B defense budget; Haaretz Jan 2026
summary: Gaza ceasefire reached mid-October 2025 after 2 years of conflict. Israel direct defense costs: ~$77B through end-2025 (Bank of Israel); U.S. military aid: $21.7B (Brown Univ). Total theater cost ~$106B to date. Post-ceasefire rate of $100M/day reflects ongoing Houthi exchanges, residual IDF brigades in Gaza, and Lebanon border operations. Iran escalation costs (Op. Epic Fury, Feb 2026+) are tracked separately. Israel approved $45B 2026 defense budget (Breaking Defense, Mar 2026).
comparables: Total military cost ($76B+) exceeds the entire GDP of Jordan — spent fighting over a territory of 2.3 million people
The 2-year war bill could have funded complete Gaza reconstruction (estimated $40–50B by the UN) almost twice over — yet rebuilding has not begun
Israel's peak daily spending during active operations ($350M+/day) exceeded the entire annual UN Relief and Works Agency (UNRWA) budget in under 3 days

casualties.source: Palestinian Ministry of Health / WAFA (Apr 1, 2026 — 72,289 killed, 172,040 injured); IMEMC News (Apr 4, 2026 — 72,291 killed, 172,068 injured); UNRWA Situation Reports 212, 214 & 216; OHCHR; Lancet Global Health (2025 — field survey ~75,200 violent deaths by Jan 5, 2025); IDF spokesperson; UN OCHA Humanitarian Situation Update #355; Al Jazeera (Feb 18, 2026 — death toll exceeds 75,000 per independent data)
casualties.note: Cumulative MoH death toll: 72,289–72,291 as of April 1–4, 2026. An estimated 673 Palestinians killed since the Oct 2025 ceasefire (UNRWA Sit Report). Independent analyses (Al Jazeera, Feb 2026) citing verified loss data put the total at over 75,000. The Lancet Global Health (2025 field survey) estimated ~75,200 violent deaths through January 5, 2025 alone — suggesting the MoH confirmed total is conservative. UNRWA has recorded 391 staff killed in Gaza since Oct 7, 2023 (to March 31, 2026). The Lancet (Jul 2024) estimated up to 186,000 total deaths including indirect causes. Since the ceasefire (Oct 10, 2025), Israel violated it at least 2,073 times through March 18, 2026. Gaza MoH figures are considered credible by WHO and UN.

entries.label: Palestinians killed (independent verified data, Al Jazeera Feb 2026)
Palestinians killed (Lancet field survey, violent deaths to Jan 5 2025)

spendChart.note: Israel direct costs + US military aid; Hamas/Houthi undisclosed

parties.name: Israel
parties.explainer: Bank of Israel reports direct defense costs of ~$77B through end-2025, covering ground operations, air campaign, Iron Dome intercepts, and reserve mobilization. Israel approved a $45B 2026 defense budget (Breaking Defense, Mar 2026) as post-ceasefire residual operations continue.

parties.name: U.S. Military Aid
parties.explainer: Brown University Costs of War: $21.7B in U.S. military aid through 2025, comprising emergency weapons packages, Iron Dome resupply, JDAM conversion kits, and naval assets deployed to the eastern Mediterranean.

parties.name: Hamas / Armed Groups
parties.explainer: Hamas operating budget is not publicly disclosed. Pre-war estimates placed Hamas military spending at $100–350M/yr — a fraction of opposing forces. Tunnel construction, weapons acquisition, and rocket production costs are not independently verifiable.

name: Sudan Civil War
parties: Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) vs. Rapid Support Forces (RSF)
sources: Nuba Reports; Taylor & Francis conflict cost study (Nov 2025); SIPRI; ACLED
summary: War erupted April 2023 between SAF and RSF. Nuba Reports: $3-8M/day in direct military spending; $8M/day used as upper-bound estimate. SAF recaptured Khartoum March 2025. 12M+ displaced. Pre-war military budget ~$1B/yr; wartime spending roughly tripled.
comparables: The daily military cost ($8M) equals the annual income of more than 10,000 Sudanese citizens — a country where GDP per capita is under $800
Sudan's wartime military spending ($3B+/yr) is roughly 15× what the government spent on healthcare before the conflict
The conflict has created the world's largest displacement crisis — at a cost that equates to $250 per displaced person per year in military spending alone

casualties.source: ACLED (documented fatalities to Nov 2024: 28,700+); OHCHR (Jan–Jun 2025: 3,384 civilians); UN OCHA; Sudan Doctors' Network; factually.co verified tally (Nov 2025); WFP (2026 — 24.6M facing acute hunger); Yale researchers warning (2026); IRC (2026 — 150,000+ killed); IRC displacement figure (2026 — 13.6M displaced); PBS NewsHour (Mar 2026 — Khartoum suffering); defconlevel.com Sudan tracker (Apr 2026)
casualties.note: Documented ACLED figures (28,700+) and aggregate estimates diverge sharply — the gap reflects unrecorded violence, famine, and disease deaths. Former U.S. envoy for Sudan stated in 2026 that as many as 400,000 may have been killed; the IRC puts the toll at 150,000+; the UN now estimates around 40,000 killed. A London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine report (Nov 2024) estimated 61,000+ deaths in Khartoum State alone for just April 2023–June 2024. Researchers note they 'can't responsibly give a number' because 'many who would do the counting are dead or displaced.' Yale researchers warned 2026 could be the conflict's bloodiest year yet. Drone strikes on hospitals, markets, and residential areas in Kordofan continue into April 2026 — April incidents include RSF drone strikes on Kartala (5 killed) and Singa (27 killed). The Sudan-origin drone that struck Chad in March 2026 (killing 17) underscores cross-border spillover. UN now reports 14 million displaced — world's largest displacement crisis. The WFP reports 24.6 million in acute hunger.

entries.label: Killed — direct violence (ACLED documented, to Nov 2024)

spendChart.note: SAF and RSF spending not independently audited

parties.name: Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF)
parties.explainer: Pre-war Sudanese military budget was ~$1B/yr. Wartime spending roughly tripled (Nuba Reports). SAF recaptured Khartoum in March 2025, suggesting sustained offensive capability. Gulf state support (UAE backing RSF, Saudi/Egypt backing SAF) complicates the picture.

parties.name: Rapid Support Forces (RSF)
parties.explainer: RSF funding draws from Sudanese gold revenues, UAE support, and captured SAF equipment — none of which is publicly audited. The RSF controlled much of Sudan's gold mining infrastructure through 2024, providing significant self-financing capacity.

name: Myanmar Civil War
parties: Military Junta (SAC) vs. People's Defence Force + ethnic armed groups
sources: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database 2024 (April 2025 release); IISS Military Balance 2026; SIPRI press release Apr 2025
summary: Junta seized power Feb 2021. SIPRI 2024 (released Apr 2025): Myanmar military spending surged 66% to $5.0B/yr — highest rate of increase in Asia-Pacific and 6.8% of GDP — equating to ~$13.7M/day. Previous $2.5B/yr figure predated the 66% surge. Military now consumes 29% of government expenditure. Resistance controls significant territory. Figures reflect junta spending only.
comparables: The junta now spends 29% of all government revenue on this war — more than education, health, and infrastructure combined
Annual military spending ($5B) is more than 10× Myanmar's entire public healthcare budget in one of Asia's poorest countries
The $13.7M/day rate means Myanmar's military spends more in a week than the entire annual UNICEF budget for the country

casualties.source: ACLED (96,000+ total killed, 2026); AAPP (Assistance Association for Political Prisoners, Apr 2025 — 6,486 confirmed civilian deaths); UN News (Jan 2026 — 5.2M displaced); OHCHR (Jan 2025 four-year report); HRW World Report 2026; Al Jazeera (Mar 27, 2026); Myanmar civil war Wikipedia (territory control estimates)
casualties.note: Myanmar's military blocks independent access. AAPP tracks confirmed civilian deaths via open-source documentation — widely considered a floor. ACLED's broader estimate of 96,000+ total killed (all parties, all causes) reflects the full scale of the conflict since the Feb 2021 coup. The junta has escalated airstrikes on civilian targets through 2025–26, including strikes on schools, hospitals, and IDP camps. A January 2026 airstrike in Bhamo Township, Kachin State killed up to 50 civilians. As of early 2026, the junta controls only 21% of Myanmar's territory, while resistance forces hold 42% (2024 investigation). The UN estimates 5.2 million people are now internally displaced (UN News, Jan 2026 — up from 3.6M in prior estimates). The March 2025 earthquake compounded the crisis: the military continued airstrikes in the immediate aftermath. Despite territorial losses, some analysts suggest the balance is beginning to tilt back toward the junta in certain regions.

spendChart.note: Junta spending only; PDF/resistance funding undisclosed

parties.name: Military Junta (SAC)
parties.explainer: SIPRI 2024 (released April 2025): Myanmar military spending surged 66% to $5.0B/yr — highest rate of increase in Asia-Pacific, consuming 29% of government expenditure and 6.8% of GDP. Previous estimate of $2.5B/yr predated this surge.

parties.name: People's Defence Force + EAOs
parties.explainer: The PDF and allied Ethnic Armed Organizations (EAOs) are funded through diaspora donations, local taxation in controlled territories, and in some cases cross-border trade. No centralized budget exists — aggregate spending is not independently verifiable.

name: Sahel / West Africa
parties: Multiple governments vs. JNIM / ISGS (Islamic State Greater Sahara)
sources: SIPRI 2024; ACLED conflict data; IISS Africa report 2025
summary: Jihadist insurgency across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, Chad. French withdrew 2023; Russian Africa Corps present. Combined government military budgets ~$1.5B/yr. ~$4M/day blended estimate. 10,000+ civilian deaths annually (ACLED).
comparables: Combined Sahel military spending ($1.5B/yr) exceeds the entire World Food Programme emergency food budget for West Africa — despite the region facing acute food insecurity
France spent more deploying its Barkhane force in its final year than all five Sahel countries combined spent on primary education
The five affected countries have per-capita GDPs of under $1,000 — yet are sustaining military expenditure that consumes up to 20% of government revenue

casualties.source: ACLED 2024 Annual Report; ACLED Africa Overview March 2026; IISS Armed Conflict Survey 2024; HRW / US News (Apr 2, 2026 — government forces killing more civilians than militants); UN OCHA (2025 — 3,737 security incidents, 9,362 deaths); Pravda Burkina Faso (Apr 4, 2026 — Sahel military summary); ACLED Conflict Watchlist 2026
casualties.note: Figures cover Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Chad. UN OCHA recorded 3,737 security incidents resulting in 9,362 deaths in the region in 2025 alone (ACLED/OCHA). The Sahel accounted for more than half of all global terrorism-related deaths in 2025 (GTI 2026). ACLED Conflict Watchlist 2026 notes the region remains the global epicenter of terrorism. A confirmed and alarming finding (HRW / ACLED / US News, April 2, 2026): government and allied forces in Burkina Faso and Mali have killed more than twice as many civilians as Islamist militants since 2023 — in Burkina Faso, government/VDP forces killed 523 civilians in 2025 vs. JNIM/ISSP killing 339; in Mali, the military and Russian Africa Corps killed 918 civilians in 2025 vs. JNIM/ISSP killing 232. JNIM launched a week-long coordinated offensive in February 2026 across eastern and northern Burkina Faso, demonstrating growing operational reach. Through March 21–April 3 (Pravda Mali summary Apr 6, 2026), fighting continued across the region. In early 2026 suspected militants killed 50+ civilians across two attacks in Niger's Tillabéri region. An oil pipeline was blown up in Niger in late March 2026. JNIM remains the primary threat; Islamic State Sahel Province (ISGS) is also active.

spendChart.note: Blended multi-country estimate; France + Russian Africa Corps included

parties.name: Sahel Governments + Partners
parties.explainer: Combined defense budgets of Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Chad total ~$1.5B/yr. France's Barkhane force (~3,000 troops, $600M/yr at peak) withdrew in 2023; Russian Africa Corps (formerly Wagner) moved in at lower cost. The G5 Sahel joint force collapsed in 2023 following the coups.

parties.name: JNIM / ISGS
parties.explainer: JNIM and ISGS are self-financing through taxation of local populations, kidnap-for-ransom, and cross-border smuggling. No reliable expenditure figure exists. JNIM controls territory spanning parts of Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger, generating significant local revenue.

name: DR Congo / M23
parties: DRC Armed Forces (FARDC) + SADC vs. M23 / Rwanda
sources: SIPRI 2023 (DRC +105%); ACLED; UN Group of Experts reports
summary: M23 resurgence from late 2022, backed by Rwanda (UN Group of Experts confirmed). DRC military spending surged 105% in 2023 — largest increase of any country globally (SIPRI). DRC defense budget ~$1.5-2B/yr by 2023. ~$5.5M/day blended estimate including SADC forces.
comparables: DRC military spending doubled in a single year (SIPRI 2023) — while per-capita healthcare spending remains around $20/year for 100 million citizens
The conflict zone contains an estimated $24 trillion in untapped mineral wealth — the entire war's cost to date is less than 0.01% of what's at stake underground
At $5.5M/day, the conflict costs more than the DRC's entire annual education budget — every 4 months

casualties.source: ACLED; UN Security Council (Aug 2025, Mar 2026); Human Rights Watch (Jun, Aug 2025); Al Jazeera DRC mapping project (Mar 2025); OHCHR (Aug 2025); African Union (Feb 12, 2026 — ceasefire TOR signing); UN MONUSCO (Mar 2026); Security Council Report April 2026 forecast
casualties.note: On February 11–12, 2026, Angola brokered a ceasefire with Terms of Reference signed. The ceasefire took effect February 18, 2026. However, the UN Security Council was briefed in March 2026 that the situation 'continues to deteriorate.' The Doha Framework for Peace (November 2025) relocated to Switzerland in April 2026. Despite diplomatic activity, ACLED warns the Islamic State is capitalizing on M23's territorial gains. As of April 1–3, 2026, M23 made limited tactical withdrawals: elements pulled back from Katondi village (~10 miles south of Lubero, Apr 2) while still holding Lubango nearby — volatility continues. Pro-Congolese Wazalendo fighters recaptured several villages in Kalehe district (border of North/South Kivu). Over 500,000 displaced in South Kivu alone since December 2025. Documented mass atrocities: 773 killed in Goma (Feb 2025); 319+ civilians in Rutshuru (Jul 2025); 400+ in South Kivu (Dec 2025). 6.4M+ internally displaced; food insecurity projected to affect 26.6M in 2026.

spendChart.note: DRC + SADC coalition; Rwanda officially denies involvement

parties.name: DRC + SADC Forces (FARDC)
parties.explainer: DRC military spending surged 105% in 2023 — the largest single-year increase of any country globally (SIPRI), driven by the M23 resurgence. DRC defense budget reached ~$1.5–2B/yr by 2023. SADC's SAMIM regional force deployed June 2021 and withdrew July 2024, adding additional coalition costs.

parties.name: M23 / Rwanda (RDF)
parties.explainer: Rwanda officially denies direct military involvement, but UN Group of Experts has confirmed Rwanda Defence Force (RDF) troops embedded with M23. Rwandan military spending ($547M/yr, SIPRI) does not disclose DRC-specific costs. M23 receives weapons, logistics, and personnel from Rwanda — the financial quantum is not independently auditable.

name: The Twelve-Day War (Israel–Iran)
parties: Israel + U.S. vs. Iran
sources: New Arab — Israel faces $12B losses after 12-day war; TRT World — total U.S. + Israel + Iran costs; Al Jazeera Centre for Studies — Twelve Days of Inferno analysis; Britannica — 12-Day War entry; House of Commons Library CBP-10292 (Impacts of June 2025 strikes)
summary: On June 13, 2025, Israel launched a massive air campaign against Iranian nuclear facilities, military bases, and IRGC infrastructure — the IDF's largest single operation, deploying 200+ fighter jets across 5 strike waves targeting 100+ sites. Iran retaliated with 550 ballistic missiles and 1,000 drones. Trump announced a ceasefire on June 23, 2025, naming it the 'Twelve-Day War.' Israel's direct losses estimated at $12B; total combined costs (Israel + U.S. support + Iranian response) estimated at $18B over 12 days (~$1.5B/day). This operation served as the tactical blueprint for Op. Epic Fury (Feb 2026).
comparables: At $1.5B/day, this was the costliest 12-day conflict in recorded history — more expensive per day than the peak of the first Gulf War
The total cost ($18B) exceeds the Manhattan Project in today's dollars ($28B) — spent in under two weeks
Israel launched 200+ jets across 5 strike waves; at average sortie costs, the air campaign alone consumed more than the IDF's entire annual equipment procurement budget

casualties.source: House of Commons Library CBP-10292; Al Jazeera Centre for Studies; Middle East Monitor; Iranian state media
casualties.note: Iran did not publish official military casualty figures. Estimates are derived from strike damage assessments and open-source intelligence.

spendChart.note: Israel direct losses ~$12B; U.S. support ~$6B over 12 days

parties.name: Israel
parties.explainer: The New Arab estimated Israel's direct military losses at $12B over 12 days — comprising munitions expenditure, air defense interceptions (Iran fired 550 ballistic missiles and 1,000 drones), and operational costs for 200+ jet sorties across 5 strike waves. IDF's largest single operation in its history.

parties.name: United States
parties.explainer: U.S. support included positioning carrier strike groups in the eastern Mediterranean and Red Sea, providing real-time intelligence sharing, and pre-positioning munitions. TRT World and Al Jazeera Centre for Studies estimated total U.S. support costs at ~$6B across the 12-day operation.

parties.name: Iran
parties.explainer: Iran's response costs — 550 ballistic missiles and 1,000 drone launches, nationwide air defense activation, IRGC operations — are not publicly disclosed. Iran's total annual IRGC budget is itself classified. No reliable figure can be modelled from open sources.

name: Yemen War
parties: Saudi-led coalition vs. Houthi forces (Ansar Allah)
sources: Wilson Center Dec 2015 ($200M/day peak); GAO 2022 ($54.6B US support 2015-2021); SIPRI
summary: Saudi-led coalition intervened March 2015. Wilson Center estimated $200M/day at peak (Dec 2015). GAO confirmed $54.6B in U.S. military support to Saudi Arabia and UAE, 2015-2021. ~$67M/day is a blended 8-year average accounting for high early intensity and reduced tempo post-2022 UN truce. UN-brokered truce April 2023.
comparables: The U.S. supplied $54.6B in confirmed military support (GAO) — enough to have funded Yemen's entire economy for a decade at pre-war GDP levels
Saudi Arabia spent more on this conflict annually than on education for its 35 million citizens — for 8 consecutive years
Total cost ($195B+) approaches the entire GDP of Qatar — the country that ultimately helped broker the ceasefire

casualties.source: ACLED; UN OCHA (377,000 est. by end-2021); Yemen Data Project; UNHCR
casualties.note: The UN estimated 377,000 total deaths by end-2021 — the majority from indirect causes: famine, disease, and collapse of healthcare infrastructure. Direct combat deaths are a fraction of the total human cost.

entries.label: Total deaths — direct + indirect (UN est. to 2021)

spendChart.note: Saudi-led coalition + U.S. support; Houthi spending undisclosed

parties.name: Saudi-Led Coalition
parties.explainer: Saudi Arabia and UAE led the coalition with an estimated $5–7B/yr in direct military costs at peak. Saudi defense spending grew from $81B (2015) to $86B (2016) — the Yemen campaign consumed an estimated 10–15% of that budget. UAE withdrew most forces by 2019; Saudi costs declined thereafter.

parties.name: U.S. Military Support
parties.explainer: GAO confirmed $54.6B in U.S. military support to Saudi Arabia and UAE for Yemen operations, 2015–2021 — comprising arms sales, mid-air refueling (until 2018), and intelligence sharing. Biden paused offensive arms sales in 2021; support effectively ended by 2022.

parties.name: Houthis (Ansar Allah)
parties.explainer: Houthi funding comes primarily from Iran (estimated $100M+/yr in weapons and support per U.S. Treasury designations) and captured Yemeni state assets. The group has no publicly disclosed budget. Missile and drone production costs benefit from Iranian technology transfer.

name: Ethiopia / Tigray War
parties: Ethiopian federal forces + Eritrea vs. TPLF / Tigray Defence Forces
sources: SIPRI (Ethiopia budget $1.03B in 2022, +111%); East African / SIPRI Feb 2022; Oxford Initiative on Peace and Recovery in Ethiopia
summary: War began Nov 2020. Ethiopia's military budget doubled to $1.03B/yr at peak (SIPRI 2022). Infrastructure damage assessed at $22.7B (Ethiopian government). ~$2.8M/day reflects wartime expenditure increase above pre-war baseline, averaged over 2-year conflict. Pretoria ceasefire Nov 2, 2022. Up to 600,000 civilians killed (researchers).
comparables: Ethiopia doubled its military budget to fight this war while simultaneously experiencing the world's largest humanitarian crisis of 2021
Infrastructure damage alone ($22.7B, Ethiopian government estimate) exceeded direct military spending by more than 8× — and reconstruction has barely begun
Up to 600,000 civilians were killed — making this one of the deadliest conflicts since WWII, at a military cost of under $3M/day

casualties.source: Ghent University (2023 peer-reviewed estimate); OHCHR; Amnesty International; Ethiopian Disaster Risk Management Commission
casualties.note: One of the most under-reported conflicts of the modern era. Ghent University's peer-reviewed estimate of 300,000–500,000 deaths (including famine and disease) is the most cited academic figure. Some researchers place the total above 600,000.

spendChart.note: Ethiopian federal + Eritrean spending; Eritrea undisclosed

parties.name: Ethiopia + Eritrea
parties.explainer: Ethiopia's military budget doubled to $1.03B/yr at peak (SIPRI 2022), driven by the Tigray campaign. Eritrea deployed forces but does not publish defense expenditure. Combined wartime spending is estimated at the equivalent of $2.8M/day — roughly the increase above Ethiopia's pre-war military baseline.

parties.name: TPLF / Tigray Defence Forces
parties.explainer: TPLF/TDF funding came from local taxation, captured federal equipment, and diaspora support — none of which is audited. The TDF fielded an estimated 250,000 fighters at peak, suggesting substantial expenditure, but no independent cost figure exists.

name: Libya Civil War
parties: GNA / GNU vs. LNA (Haftar) + foreign backers (Turkey, UAE, Russia, Egypt)
sources: SIPRI; IISS; Stockholm Forum on Peace and Development
summary: Second Libyan Civil War began 2014. Multiple foreign powers: Turkey backed GNA (~$2B arms/forces), UAE/Russia/Egypt backed LNA (Haftar). ~$8M/day blended estimate covering all parties including foreign military presence. UN-brokered ceasefire Oct 2020; situation remains frozen with divided government.
comparables: At least 6 foreign nations funded proxy forces in Libya — with Turkey, UAE, and Russia each spending more annually than Libya's own oil revenue in low-output years
The total cost ($17.5B over 6 years) is less than what Saudi Arabia spent on its military in a single month during the same period
Libya has some of Africa's largest oil reserves — yet the war cost more than the country earned from oil exports in its lowest-production years

casualties.source: ACLED; UNSMIL (UN Support Mission in Libya); Libya Body Count
casualties.note: The chaotic multi-faction nature of the conflict and lack of central record-keeping makes precise figures impossible. Most casualties were combatants from multiple factions.

spendChart.note: Multi-proxy conflict; Turkey, UAE, Russia, Egypt costs estimated

parties.name: GNA / GNU + Turkey
parties.explainer: Turkey committed an estimated $2B+ in arms, drones, and military personnel to back the GNA (later GNU). Turkish involvement escalated significantly in 2019–2020 with the deployment of Syrian mercenaries and Bayraktar TB2 drones — the first large-scale combat use of armed drones in the region.

parties.name: LNA (Haftar) + UAE/Russia/Egypt
parties.explainer: UAE provided Haftar's LNA with advanced weaponry including Chinese-made drones, in violation of the UN arms embargo. Russia deployed Wagner Group mercenaries (estimated 1,000–2,000 at peak). Egypt provided air support and training. Combined proxy spending estimated at $5–10B over the conflict — none publicly audited.

name: Syrian Civil War
parties: Assad government + Russia + Iran vs. opposition + ISIS + U.S. coalition
sources: SIPRI; Brown University Costs of War; Syrian Observatory for Human Rights; Watson Institute
summary: 13-year civil war. At peak (2013-2016): Assad forces + Russia (~$4M/day) + Iran + U.S. coalition (~$14M/day for ISIS campaign) exceeded $50M/day combined. ~$35M/day is a conflict-lifetime blended average accounting for reduced tempo post-2019. Assad regime fell December 8, 2024.
comparables: The 13-year conflict destroyed more than $400B in Syrian economic output — roughly 7× the total direct military cost tracked here
World Bank estimates $400B is needed to rebuild Syria — the international community has pledged less than 10% of that since 2011
At peak spending ($50M+/day in 2015-16), the war burned through a NASA annual budget ($24.8B) every 18 months — while Syrian GDP collapsed by 60%

casualties.source: Syrian Observatory for Human Rights (SOHR); UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs; Brown University Costs of War
casualties.note: SOHR is the most comprehensive single tracker, though its methodology is disputed. The UN suspended official death counts in 2014 citing data quality; independent estimates range from 350,000 to 600,000+.

spendChart.note: Assad regime + Russia vs. US-led coalition; opposition/ISIS undisclosed

parties.name: Assad Regime + Russia + Iran
parties.explainer: Russia's direct military intervention (from Sept 2015) cost an estimated $4–6M/day at peak. Iran spent an estimated $6B+/yr supporting Assad through IRGC advisors, Hezbollah deployment, and weapons transfers (CSIS). Assad's own military budget declined as his army shrank from 300,000 to ~100,000.

parties.name: U.S.-Led Coalition
parties.explainer: Operation Inherent Resolve (Aug 2014–2019 in Syria) cost $24.7B in confirmed U.S. spending (CRS). At peak, the Pentagon reported ~$14M/day in U.S. costs alone. UK, France, Australia, and others added further coalition contributions. Costs sharply declined after ISIS's territorial defeat in March 2019.

parties.name: Opposition / ISIS / Kurds
parties.explainer: The Syrian opposition was funded by Gulf states (Qatar, Saudi Arabia), Turkey, and the CIA's Timber Sycamore program (~$1B total). ISIS self-funded through oil, extortion, and taxes on occupied territories at an estimated $1–2M/day at peak. No aggregate auditable figure exists.

name: Afghanistan War (2011-2021)
parties: U.S. / NATO / Afghan forces vs. Taliban / al-Qaeda
sources: Brown University Costs of War Project; SIGAR; Congressional Research Service; SIPRI
summary: Tracking from 2011 to U.S. withdrawal Aug 2021. Brown University Costs of War: total U.S. cost ~$2.3T over full conflict (2001-2021). ~$116M/day reflects 2011-2021 average of ~$42B/yr in direct DoD war appropriations plus coalition. Peak spending ~$120B/yr (2010-2012). Taliban retook Kabul Aug 15, 2021.
comparables: Brown University estimates the full lifecycle cost at $2.3T (2001–2021, including veteran care) — roughly equivalent to the entire annual GDP of Italy, the world's 8th largest economy
The U.S. spent $116M/day for 10 years in a country whose entire GDP never exceeded $21B/yr — spending more in 6 months than Afghanistan produced in a year
Peak U.S. spending ($120B/yr, 2010-12) exceeded the entire NASA budget nearly 5× — every single year — yet produced no lasting strategic outcome

casualties.source: U.S. Department of Defense (confirmed U.S./coalition deaths); SIGAR; Brown University Costs of War; UNAMA (civilian deaths)
casualties.note: Brown University's full accounting (2001–2021) includes the pre-2011 period not tracked here. Afghan security force and Taliban figures are estimates — no authoritative record exists.

spendChart.note: US + NATO direct war costs 2011–2021; Taliban undisclosed

parties.name: United States
parties.explainer: Direct DoD war appropriations averaged ~$42B/yr (2011–2021) per the Congressional Research Service, covering combat operations, Afghan security force training, and base infrastructure. Peak spending was ~$120B/yr in 2010–2012, declining as U.S. forces drew down from 100,000 to 2,500 troops by the 2021 withdrawal.

parties.name: NATO + Afghan Forces
parties.explainer: NATO non-US allies contributed ~$12B+ in direct war costs and training. The Afghan National Security Forces (ANSF) were funded primarily through the Afghan Security Forces Fund (~$5–6B/yr U.S. appropriation) plus Afghan domestic budget contributions. SIGAR tracked over $90B in total U.S. reconstruction aid including ANSF funding.

parties.name: Taliban
parties.explainer: Taliban funding derives from opium/heroin production (the group taxed ~90% of global heroin supply), extortion, mining, and donations from Gulf-based supporters. Estimates range from $300M to $1.5B/yr — none independently audited.

name: Nagorno-Karabakh Wars
parties: Azerbaijan (+ Turkey) vs. Armenia / Artsakh forces
sources: SIPRI; IISS; Armenian and Azerbaijani defense ministry budget data; Reuters
summary: 44-Day War (Sep-Nov 2020) followed by Azerbaijan's final offensive in Sept 2023. Azerbaijan military spending ~$2.65B/yr (SIPRI 2020); Armenia ~$630M/yr. Combined ~$3.3B/yr or ~$9M/day at peak. Blended ~$12M/day including Turkish drone operations and advisor costs. Azerbaijan seized full control Sept 19, 2023; ethnic Armenian population fled.
comparables: The 44-Day War (2020) was the first conflict to demonstrate drone warfare at decisive scale — Azerbaijan spent less on drones than a single U.S. destroyer, yet achieved strategic victory
Total conflict cost ($11B over 3 years) is less than the price of one Gerald R. Ford-class aircraft carrier ($13B) — yet permanently redrew the map of the South Caucasus
Azerbaijan's military buildup was funded almost entirely by oil revenues — it spent more on defense modernization in 5 years than Armenia's entire GDP

casualties.source: Official government figures (Azerbaijan and Armenia); ICRC; Reuters
casualties.note: Both governments published official figures for the 44-Day War. The 2023 offensive figures are less independently verified. Civilian casualties were relatively low given the intensity of combat.

spendChart.note: Azerbaijan + Turkey vs. Armenia/Artsakh; two distinct conflict phases

parties.name: Azerbaijan (+ Turkey)
parties.explainer: Azerbaijan's military budget was $2.65B/yr (SIPRI 2020), entirely funded by oil revenues, after years of buildup following the First Nagorno-Karabakh War. Turkish advisory support and Bayraktar TB2 drone systems were decisive in the 44-Day War. The 2023 offensive was brief (24 hours of decisive operations) and far less costly.

parties.name: Armenia / Artsakh
parties.explainer: Armenia's defense budget was ~$630M/yr (SIPRI 2020), less than a quarter of Azerbaijan's. Artsakh (Nagorno-Karabakh) had a separate defense force funded by Armenian diaspora and Russian equipment transfers. The 2023 offensive collapsed Artsakh's defenses in under 24 hours, ending Armenian presence in the territory.

name: War Against ISIS (Iraq/Syria)
parties: U.S.-led coalition + Iraq + Kurdish forces vs. Islamic State (ISIS/ISIL)
sources: Pentagon Cost of War reports; Congressional Research Service; Watson Institute; Defense One
summary: ISIS declared caliphate June 2014; Operation Inherent Resolve began Aug 2014. Pentagon reported ~$14M/day in U.S. costs alone at peak (2016). Full coalition including Iraq, Kurdish, French, UK operations ~$25M/day. Total U.S. cost ~$24.7B (CRS). ISIS territorial defeat at Baghouz, Syria, March 23, 2019.
comparables: ISIS at its peak self-funded on roughly $1–2M/day through oil smuggling and extortion — the coalition spent 12× that amount daily to defeat them
The U.S. alone launched 34,000+ airstrikes against ISIS — at an average cost of ~$700,000 per strike, totaling $24.7B in confirmed U.S. spending
The total coalition cost ($47B+ combined) exceeds what ISIS controlled in annual oil revenue by 20× — illustrating the profound asymmetry of modern counter-insurgency

casualties.source: Pentagon / Operation Inherent Resolve; Airwars (civilian airstrike deaths); ACLED; IBC (Iraq Body Count); CRS
casualties.note: ISIS fighter deaths are Pentagon estimates and may be overstated. Civilian deaths from coalition airstrikes are tracked by Airwars — their figures are considered more reliable than coalition self-reporting.

spendChart.note: US-led coalition vs. Iraqi/Kurdish forces; ISIS self-funded

parties.name: U.S.-Led Coalition
parties.explainer: Total confirmed U.S. cost: $24.7B (Congressional Research Service), covering 34,000+ airstrikes and Operation Inherent Resolve ground advisors. Pentagon reported ~$14M/day in U.S.-only costs at peak (2016). UK, France, Canada, and Australia added further coalition contributions, bringing the total to ~$35B.

parties.name: Iraq + Kurdish Forces (ISF/Peshmerga)
parties.explainer: Iraqi Security Forces and Kurdish Peshmerga bore the majority of ground combat against ISIS. Iraq's defense budget grew from $6B to $14B/yr during the campaign. The Kurdish Regional Government's Peshmerga received ~$800M in U.S. equipment and training. Combined ground force costs estimated at ~$9M/day average.

parties.name: ISIS / ISIL
parties.explainer: At its peak (2014–2015), ISIS self-funded at an estimated $1–2M/day through oil smuggling (~$1.5M/day), extortion, taxation, and looting — one of the most financially sophisticated terrorist organizations in history. Revenue collapsed as territory was lost.

name: Nigeria / Boko Haram + ISWAP
parties: Nigeria + MNJTF vs. Boko Haram / ISWAP
sources: SIPRI Military Expenditure Database 2020–2024 (Nigeria defense spending: $2.57B 2020, $4.47B 2021, $3.11B 2022, $3.2B 2023); ISS Africa — conflict financing analysis; Ecofin Agency (SIPRI Nigeria +56% YoY 2021); ACLED Nigeria country data; CFR Boko Haram's Deadly Impact
summary: Boko Haram's violent insurgency erupted July 2009 in Maiduguri, northeastern Nigeria. The group fractured in 2016 — Boko Haram (JAS) weakened, ISWAP (Islamic State West Africa Province) expanded. Nigeria's defense budget grew from ~$1.5B/yr in 2009 to $4.47B in 2021 (SIPRI), driven substantially by the northeast campaign. Nigeria also leads the Multinational Joint Task Force (MNJTF) with Cameroon, Chad, and Niger. Approximately 40–50% of Nigeria's defense spending — plus MNJTF contributions — is attributed to the northeast theater, averaging ~$2.5M/day over the conflict's lifetime. ISWAP now controls larger territory than Boko Haram ever did. 50,000+ killed; 2M+ IDPs in Borno, Yobe, and Adamawa states.
comparables: Nigeria's annual northeast security cost exceeds the entire national budget for universal basic education — for a country where 10 million children are out of school
The total estimated cost ($15B+) is more than twice Nigeria's peak annual oil revenue during the 2016 oil-price crash — spent fighting an insurgency that has lasted longer than WWII
The Lake Chad Basin humanitarian crisis (10.7M+ affected) costs the international community more to respond to each year than Nigeria spends fighting the insurgency itself

casualties.source: ACLED Nigeria (34,261 documented conflict deaths Jun 2011–Jun 2018); CFR Boko Haram's Deadly Impact (50,000+ total cited); Global Centre for R2P; ISS Africa; UNHCR; HRW World Report 2026; Critical Threats Africa File (Feb 5, 2026); International Christian Concern (Mar 30, 2026)
casualties.note: ACLED documented 34,261 killed in 2011–2018 alone. The total since 2009 is widely cited as 50,000–60,000+. ISWAP has intensified operations significantly in 2025–26: daily attacks on civilians and security forces in Yobe and Borno states. Notable 2026 incidents include: a massacre of 160–170 civilians in two neighboring Kwara State villages on February 3, 2026 (one of the deadliest single attacks in years); the abduction of 300+ civilians in Ngoshe, Borno State in early March 2026; suspected suicide bombings in northeastern Nigeria in March 2026 killing 23 people and wounding 108; and a Palm Sunday attack in Ungwan Rukuba, Jos North (Plateau State) on March 30, 2026, killing at least 30 people. Jihadists intensified direct attacks against the Nigerian military in March 2026, killing officers and seizing weapons (Washington Post, Mar 10, 2026). Nigeria's armed forces are now deployed in two-thirds of all states — severely overstretched. Civilian deaths remain heavily undercounted given severe access restrictions.

entries.label: Lake Chad Basin — total humanitarian crisis (affected)

spendChart.note: Nigerian defense budget estimate; Boko Haram/ISWAP spending undisclosed

parties.name: Nigeria + MNJTF
parties.explainer: Nigeria's defense budget grew from ~$1.5B/yr (2009) to $4.47B (2021, SIPRI) — a 3× increase driven substantially by the northeast campaign. The Multinational Joint Task Force (MNJTF), incorporating Cameroon, Chad, and Niger, adds further regional costs. An estimated 40–50% of Nigeria's defense budget is attributable to the Boko Haram/ISWAP theater.

parties.name: Boko Haram / ISWAP
parties.explainer: Boko Haram and its ISWAP splinter are self-financing through kidnap-for-ransom, cattle rustling, taxation of Lake Chad fishing communities, and Islamic State global network support. ISWAP now controls larger territory than Boko Haram ever did, generating substantial local revenue — no reliable budget exists.

name: Somalia / Al-Shabaab War
parties: Somalia + AU Mission (AMISOM/ATMIS/AUSSOM) + U.S. vs. Al-Shabaab
sources: ISS Africa (ATMIS annual cost $1.5B in 2023); IPI — Lessons from AMISOM (2019, initial budget $622M); EU Commission (€4.3B committed since 2007); IPI Global Observatory — AUSSOM $196M 2025 budget; CFR Global Conflict Tracker; ACLED Somalia; VOA — Al-Shabaab 4,000+ civilian deaths
summary: AMISOM deployed March 2007 to support Somalia's Transitional Federal Government. Mission peaked at 22,000 troops, grew from $350M/yr to ~$1B/yr (AMISOM), then $1.5B/yr (ATMIS, 2022–2023). The EU has invested €4.3B ($4.7B) in Somalia security since 2007; U.S. spent ~$2B in counter-terrorism assistance 2007–2020 with ongoing airstrikes. Al-Shabaab controls significant rural territory despite 18 years of operations. Current mission (AUSSOM, 2025) budgeted at just $196M — a severe reduction that threatens the mission's viability. Al-Shabaab remains the most prolific Al-Qaeda affiliate by attack volume. $2M/day reflects a mission-lifetime average across escalating and de-escalating phases.
comparables: The EU alone has invested €4.3 billion in Somalia's security since 2007 — enough to reconstruct the entire country's road network three times over
Al-Shabaab remains the world's most active Al-Qaeda affiliate despite 18 years of AU peacekeeping and $16B+ in external security spending — illustrating the limits of military solutions to insurgency
AMISOM at peak deployed 22,000 troops — more soldiers than the entire standing armies of 70 UN member states — yet could not permanently dislodge Al-Shabaab from rural Somalia

casualties.source: ACLED Somalia (4,000+ civilian deaths from Al-Shabaab attacks, 2010–2021, per VOA); ACLED 2024 Al-Shabaab activity mapping; Mogadishu Medina Hospital data (54,000+ injuries since 2007); CFR Al-Shabaab conflict tracker; UNHCR; CTC West Point — Somalia at a Crossroads (2026); UN Security Council Resolution 2809 (2025); AU Peace and Security Council communiqué Feb 12, 2026
casualties.note: ACLED's civilian-targeting figures (4,000+ since 2010) are a floor. The AUSSOM mission was re-authorized by the UN Security Council to December 31, 2026, with up to 11,826 uniformed personnel. The severe funding reduction from ATMIS ($1.5B/yr) to AUSSOM ($196M budget) threatens mission viability. Al-Shabaab lost its leader in a U.S.-enabled kill announced in February 2026 but the group remains resilient — AUSSOM has denied Al-Shabaab's competing casualty claims. Somali forces and AU troops killed 40+ Al-Shabaab militants in a single Lower Shabelle engagement at Mubarak (March 2026, Axadle/AUSSOM). On April 2, 2026, a joint AUSSOM-SNAF operation captured senior Al-Shabaab battalion commander Salaad Cusmaan Macalin ('Sahm'), accompanied by 17 foreign fighters from Al-Shabaab's Jilib command center — one of the most significant commander captures in recent months. 30 militants were killed in a January 2026 joint operation in Lower Shabelle. AUSSOM was reduced from 22,000+ to roughly 12,000 troops in 2025. 2022 was the deadliest year since 2018, with 6,500+ fatalities (ACLED).

spendChart.note: AMISOM/ATMIS/AUSSOM mission lifetime average; Al-Shabaab undisclosed

parties.name: Somalia + AU Mission (AMISOM → AUSSOM)
parties.explainer: AMISOM peaked at 22,000 troops with a budget of ~$1B/yr, funded primarily by the EU (€4.3B committed since 2007) and U.S. counter-terrorism assistance (~$2B over 2007–2020). Mission transitioned to ATMIS ($1.5B/yr, 2022–2023) then dramatically contracted to AUSSOM ($196M budget, 2025) — an 87% funding cut that severely threatens operational capacity.

parties.name: Al-Shabaab
parties.explainer: Al-Shabaab self-finances through port taxation at Kismayo and other towns, extortion of businesses and NGOs, and international Al-Qaeda network transfers. The UN Monitoring Group estimated revenues of $50–100M/yr — making it one of the wealthiest non-state armed groups in Africa. No auditable budget exists.

name: South Sudan Civil War
parties: SPLA / government forces (Kiir) + Uganda vs. SPLM-IO (Machar) + UNMISS (UN peacekeeping)
sources: SIPRI (South Sudan military 2012: $612M / 8.4% GDP); CFR Global Conflict Tracker — South Sudan; CRS / FAS IN10975 (nearly 400,000 estimated dead); Watson Institute Costs of War; UNMISS budget data (~$1B/yr UN peacekeeping); UNHCR; OCHA
summary: Civil war erupted December 15, 2013 after a political rupture between President Salva Kiir and Vice President Riek Machar. Uganda deployed forces in support of Kiir 2013–2015 (estimated $600–800M/yr). UNMISS expanded to 17,000 troops with UN budget of ~$1B/yr throughout the conflict. South Sudan's own military spending ranged from $400M–$800M/yr. Combined direct military cost estimated at $3.5M/day average. Transitional Government of National Unity formed February 22, 2020, ending the primary conflict phase. Approximately 400,000 excess deaths; 4M+ IDPs; 2.5M+ refugees.
comparables: The war killed approximately 400,000 people — equivalent to the entire population of New Orleans — in a country of just 11 million, making this one of the deadliest post-Cold War conflicts
UNMISS alone cost the UN ~$1B/year — more than all infrastructure investment in South Sudan since its independence in 2011
In 2016, the UN estimated 65% of South Sudan's population needed emergency food aid — while both warring factions spent millions per day on arms and soldiers

casualties.source: Harvard School of Public Health / London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine (2018 peer-reviewed excess death estimate: 383,000); CRS IN10975 (citing ~400,000); UNHCR; OCHA; Amnesty International
casualties.note: The ~383,000 figure (Harvard/LSHTM, 2018 peer-reviewed) is an excess death estimate through mid-2018 covering all causes — combat, disease, and starvation. Total deaths through the 2020 peace deal are almost certainly higher. Direct combat deaths are estimated at roughly half the total; the remainder from famine and disease caused by the conflict. Access restrictions severely limited independent verification throughout.

entries.label: Excess deaths — all causes (Harvard/LSHTM est. to 2018)
Deaths from famine and disease (indirect, est.)

spendChart.note: Combined SPLA, SPLM-IO, Uganda, and UNMISS peacekeeping costs

parties.name: SPLA / Government + Uganda
parties.explainer: South Sudan's military spending peaked at $800M/yr (SIPRI), consuming 60%+ of government revenue during the conflict's worst phases. Uganda deployed forces in support of President Kiir (2013–2015), estimated at $600–800M total. UNMISS (UN peacekeeping) operated at ~$1B/yr — the largest single cost component — separately funded by UN member states.

parties.name: SPLM-IO (Machar) + Allies
parties.explainer: Riek Machar's SPLM-IO was supported by South Sudanese diaspora, captured government equipment, and reportedly Sudan's intelligence services. The group had no formal budget — funding came through looting, cattle raiding, and external patronage. No independent cost figure exists.

name: Mozambique / Cabo Delgado
parties: Mozambique + Rwanda vs. Ansar al-Sunna (ISIS-Mozambique / al-Shabaab)
sources: ACLED / Club of Mozambique (6,515 deaths Mar 2026); ISS Africa — SAMIM withdrawal analysis (May 2024); ISS Africa — Rwanda withdrawal threat analysis (Apr 2026); EU Council European Peace Facility (€20M for RDF, Nov 2024 — top-up); Irish Times (Mar 19, 2026 — EU pulls Rwanda funding); DefenceWeb (Apr 2026 — lessons from withdrawal threat); Rwanda government statement (Apr 3, 2026 — 6,300 troops, 'solidarity not money'); fundsforNGOs (Apr 3, 2026 — withdrawal scenario analysis); US sanctions on Rwanda Defence Force (Mar 2, 2026); TotalEnergies LNG resumption (Jan 2026)
summary: ISIS-linked insurgency (Ansar al-Sunna, locally called al-Shabaab) erupted October 5, 2017 in Mocímboa da Praia, Cabo Delgado. SADC deployed SAMIM in June 2021 (~$10M/month); Rwanda deployed from July 2021, now confirmed at 6,300+ troops (3× original deployment). SAMIM withdrew July 2024. EU European Peace Facility committed ~€46M total 2021–2025 — ~17% of Rwanda's costs. In March 2026, Rwanda threatened withdrawal if adequate funding not secured. The EU announced it will end Rwanda funding in May 2026. On April 3, Rwanda clarified it is there 'for solidarity, not money' (Rwanda spokesman Makolo) — tempering the withdrawal threat. However, the US imposed sanctions on the Rwanda Defence Force on March 2, 2026, complicating Rwanda's international standing and creating additional pressure. The EU indicated it may define a new 'possible support in security' mechanism by May. TotalEnergies resumed work on its $20B LNG project (Jan 2026), which Rwanda's presence helped enable. Insurgent strength estimated at 300–1,000 fighters. $600K/day reflects conflict-lifetime average; current operational rate with 6,300+ Rwandan troops is substantially higher.
comparables: Cabo Delgado sits above one of the world's largest natural gas reserves — TotalEnergies' $20B LNG project remains suspended, costing far more in lost investment than the entire war
Rwanda has committed hundreds of millions and thousands of troops to stabilize a neighbor's insurgency — while relying on EU support that has now been terminated with no replacement funding
The 6,500+ documented deaths make this one of Africa's least-covered active conflicts — despite the same region hosting a Western LNG investment larger than the entire Mozambican GDP

casualties.source: ACLED / Club of Mozambique (6,257 deaths — 8-year total to Oct 2025); ACLED Mozambique Conflict Monitor (6,515 total — Mar 2026); UNHCR; OCHA; Africanews (Mar 15, 2026 — Rwanda withdrawal threat); Zitamar Conflict Monitor (Mar 9–22, 2026); ACLED Conflict Monitor Update Mar 25, 2026
casualties.note: Of the 6,515 ACLED-documented deaths, at least 2,631 were civilians. Rwanda has 6,300+ troops deployed — confirmed by the Rwandan government on April 3, 2026. Rwanda threatened withdrawal in March 2026 after the EU confirmed it will end European Peace Facility funding in May 2026, with no replacement secured. Rwanda subsequently clarified it is present 'for solidarity, not money' (spokesman Makolo, Apr 3, 2026) — though the funding crisis remains unresolved. On March 2, 2026, the US imposed sanctions on the Rwanda Defence Force and senior officials, adding further pressure to Rwanda's calculus. Multiple outlets are now publishing withdrawal-scenario assessments. Mozambique urged the EU to reconsider; the EU indicated it may define a new security support mechanism by May. TotalEnergies resumed its $20B LNG project in January 2026 — which Rwanda's presence helped enable. The March 15 FADM killing of 13 civilians on fishing boats near Calugo drew condemnation. Insurgent membership estimated at 300–1,000 fighters.

spendChart.note: Mozambique + Rwanda costs; insurgent spending undisclosed

parties.name: Mozambique + Rwanda (RDF)
parties.explainer: Rwanda deployed 6,300+ troops (confirmed April 2026) — 3× its original commitment — enabling TotalEnergies to resume its $20B LNG project. EU European Peace Facility contributed ~€46M total (2021–2025), covering roughly 17% of Rwanda's costs. The EU announced it will end funding in May 2026. Mozambique's own defense budget is ~$95M/yr.

parties.name: Ansar al-Sunna (ISIS-Mozambique)
parties.explainer: The insurgency (locally called al-Shabaab, distinct from Somalia) is estimated at 300–1,000 active fighters. Funding derives from local taxation, gem and timber smuggling from Cabo Delgado's rich natural resources, and Islamic State network connections. No independent budget figure exists.

name: Pakistan–Afghanistan War 2026
parties: Pakistan vs. Afghan Taliban (Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan)
sources: Military.com (Feb 27, 2026 — Pakistan declares open war, airstrikes on Kabul and Kandahar); Geo.tv Operation Ghazab lil-Haq live updates; War on the Rocks (Mar 2026 — Can Pakistan's escalation compel a Taliban recalculation?); Pakistan Today (Mar 26, 2026 — Operation resumes, Torkham border reopens); Times of Islamabad (Mar 25, 2026 — Pakistan shifts battlefield strategy); Wikipedia — 2026 Afghanistan–Pakistan conflict
summary: On February 27, 2026, Pakistan launched Operation Ghazab lil-Haq ('Wrath for Justice') — a large-scale air and ground campaign against Afghan Taliban positions across multiple provinces including Kabul, Kandahar, Paktia, Nangarhar, Khost, and Paktika. Pakistan cited the Taliban's sheltering of Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP), which had carried out attacks inside Pakistan. Pakistan struck 22+ locations and claims 51 total target sites hit in airstrikes. Pakistan secured 32 sq km of strategic Afghan territory (Ghudwana enclave) in the Zhob sector. The operation has continued into late March with resumed strikes after a brief pause. Pakistan's $10B/yr defense budget (~$27M/day total) supports a major multi-front air and ground campaign; $5M/day is a conservative operational cost estimate, though reliable independent cost data is not yet available. Cost data will be revised as more authoritative sourcing emerges.
comparables: Pakistan is fighting on two fronts simultaneously — the Taliban across the Durand Line, and TTP insurgents inside its own borders — with a military budget smaller than what India spends in 3 weeks
The Durand Line — the disputed border at the heart of this conflict — was drawn by a British colonial administrator in 1893; Afghanistan has never formally recognised it
Pakistan struck Kabul and Kandahar — cities of 4M+ and 600,000 people respectively — while itself hosting one of the world's largest Afghan refugee populations (1.3M+ registered)

casualties.source: Pakistani military statements (Mar–Apr 2026 — 435+ Taliban killed, 630+ wounded claimed); Afghan Taliban statements (disputed); Military.com (Feb 27, 2026); Pakistan Today (Mar 26, 2026); UN OCHA Afghanistan Situation Updates #2 and #3 (Apr 5, 2026); Al Jazeera (Feb 27, 2026 — contradictory death tolls); OHCHR (Mar 2026 — piles misery on misery); Kabul Tribune (Apr 2026 — 94,000+ displaced)
casualties.note: Both sides release sharply contradictory figures. OCHA Situation Update #3 (Apr 5, 2026): over 94,000 people (13,400+ families) displaced across Khost (26,200), Kunar (25,300), Nangarhar (19,600), Paktia (18,200), and Paktika (4,900). Taliban officials stated that between Feb 22 and April 3, at least 761 civilians were killed and 626 injured in attacks attributed to Pakistani forces, with Pakistani forces firing ~15,000 mortar rounds and rockets. Pakistan maintains its strikes targeted militant infrastructure. Pakistani official claim: 435+ Taliban/fighters killed, 630+ wounded as of late March. Afghan Defence Ministry estimated ~150 Pakistani troops killed — contested by Pakistan. A temporary Eid ceasefire (Mar 18/19 – Mar 23/24) was observed; operations resumed after. Pakistan successfully brokered the Iran-U.S. ceasefire in April 2026 (Islamabad Accords), demonstrating diplomatic reach despite the simultaneous border war.

entries.label: Afghan Taliban / fighters (KIA, Pakistani military claim)
Afghan civilians killed (Taliban claim, Feb 22 – Apr 3)
Afghan civilians injured (Taliban claim, Feb 22 – Apr 3)

spendChart.note: Pakistani operational costs; Taliban expenditure undisclosed

parties.name: Pakistan (PAF + Army)
parties.explainer: Pakistan's defense budget is ~$10B/yr ($27M/day total). Operation Ghazab lil-Haq involved air strikes on 22+ locations, ground operations securing 32 sq km of Afghan territory, and sustained artillery and rocket fire — estimated at $5M/day in operational costs above Pakistan's baseline military spending.

parties.name: Afghan Taliban (IEA)
parties.explainer: The Islamic Emirate of Afghanistan has no publicly disclosed defense budget. Taliban revenues derive from opium/heroin taxation (~$400–600M/yr per UNODC), mining royalties, customs duties, and international aid diverted through the Afghan banking system. Military expenditure is not separately reported.
